sections.label: Title
sections.html:
Visible Reasoning.

How connected logs and ranked evidence turn an AI’s answer into something you can trust.

footprintjs · the self-explaining stack

sections.label: Era One
sections.html:
THREE ERAS OF TRUST

Every era asked trust a new question.

When machines replaced human work
RELIABILITY
“Did it perform the task correctly?”
metrics · logs · audits ✓
Machines had to prove their work — and they could: measure it, log it, audit it.

sections.label: Era Two
sections.html:
THREE ERAS OF TRUST

Each question found its machinery.

When the internet connected the world
PRIVACY
“Can anyone access my data?”
consent systems · encryption ✓
Privacy got machinery too: consent systems and encryption. Trust, both times, was earned by construction.

sections.label: Era Three
sections.html:
A QUESTION WITH NO MACHINERY YET

Generative AI doesn’t just automate. It reasons.

When generative AI began to reason
REASONING
“Can we trust how the system thinks?”
?
“Can we trust how the system thinks?” — the first trust question without an established answer.

sections.label: Response + Reasoning
sections.html:
WHAT THE SYSTEM SHIPS CHANGED

The product is now the answer and the thinking.

YESTERDAY · RESPONSE
“Route the ticket to billing.”
You checked the answer, and that was enough.
verify the output ✓
TODAY · RESPONSE + REASONING
“Route the ticket to billing.”
1read the ticket
2matched a refund intent
3chose the billing queue
Now the thinking ships too — and it needs to be trusted.
WHAT to build → observability ✓HOW to build it → ?
Observability names what to capture. The open question is how the record earns a person’s trust.

sections.label: The Question
sections.html:
A STUDENT ASKS

One question, in good faith.

FRONT END · THE CHAT
BACK END · TOOLS & DATA
Hi! How can I help you today?
What career path should I pursue?
A student asks an AI assistant for career advice. Watch the backend — that’s where this story lives.

sections.label: The Fetches
sections.html:
RUN 1

Two sources in, one answer out.

run 1
schoolData(123) ✓socialMedia(123) ✓
You could be a great researcher.
The backend reads school data and social media, and the answer sounds right. The student moves on.

sections.label: Two Minutes Later
sections.html:
RUN 2 · TWO MINUTES LATER

Same question. One source this time.

—  TWO MINUTES LATER  —
What career path should I pursue?
run 2
socialMedia(123) ✓schoolData(123) — never called
You could be a leader — maybe President.
schoolData never gets called — a timeout, a cache, a flaky tool. And the career flips to President.

sections.label: The Flip
sections.html:
THE MOMENT TRUST BREAKS

“How did my career flip in two minutes?”

How did my career flip in two minutes?
Both answers were fluent and confident. From the outside, nothing shows why they differ.

sections.label: What Went Wrong
sections.html:
THE DEVELOPER DIGS

The second answer never saw the school data.

EACH ANSWER ↔ ITS DATA
the developer digs
through the record
run 1 · read
schoolData ✓  socialMedia ✓
→ “Researcher”
run 2 · read
socialMedia ✓  schoolData —
→ “President”
run 2 never saw schoolData.
Visible only because each answer is linked to the data it used. Most logs never keep that link.

sections.label: Many Agents
sections.html:
MORE THAN ONE MIND

Now split the mind across organizations.

the student
Career Agent
orchestrator
SCHOOL ORG
School Agent
schoolData
grades · fair · magazine
MEDIA ORG
Media Agent
socialMedia
likes · posts · talks
Do we dig through every org’s logs separately?
Two agents, two organizations, one orchestrator. The link you need runs between them.

sections.label: Connected Logs
sections.html:
ONE CONNECTED RECORD

Connect the logs across the agents.

step · reasoning + data ✓step · reasoning + data ✓step · reasoning + data ✓
One trace, threaded through every agent. Each step keeps two things: the reasoning, and the data that step used.

sections.label: Show The Evidence
sections.html:
TRUST FORMS AT PRESENTATION

Show the evidence with the answer.

“You could be a robotics scientist.”
REASONING STEP · GRANDPARENT
they build and present original projects
REASONING STEP · PARENT
hands-on engineering keeps recurring
EVIDENCE · d
School · won the robotics fair
THE CANDIDATES
YOUTUBE
liked leaders’ & robotics talks
LINKEDIN
robotics posts · an IEEE talk
SCHOOL
won the robotics fair
SCHOOL
played baseball for a season
The connected log knows which data each step used. Four candidates back this answer — which mattered most? Sort them.

sections.label: Sp
sections.html:
SCORE ONE PIECE OF EVIDENCE

Sp — how close is it to the step that used it?

Sp = .86
Sp .86
Take the fair win. Its parent step reads “hands-on engineering keeps recurring” — similarity .86.

sections.label: Sgp
sections.html:
ONE HOP FURTHER UP

Sgp — and to the step above that?

Sgp = .79
Sgp .79
One hop up the chain, the evidence still tracks the reasoning: .79. Two similarities, one per ancestor.

sections.label: Relevancy
sections.html:
TERM ONE · RELEVANCY

Relevancy — the average across its ancestors.

R = avg = .83
R = (Sp + Sgp) / 2. Did this evidence stay on topic all the way up — or drift?

sections.label: Persistence
sections.html:
TERM TWO · PERSISTENCE

Persistence — does it keep clearing the bar?

P = #{s > T} = 2/2
Count the ancestors above threshold T. A spike mentions you once — persistence means the chain kept leaning on it.

sections.label: Depth
sections.html:
TERM THREE · DEPTH

Depth — how far from the final answer?

3 hops
D̂ = near = .75
Distance from d to the answer. Evidence used next to the answer speaks louder than evidence buried at the start.

sections.label: The Score
sections.html:
ONE NUMBER PER EVIDENCE

Normalize, weight, add: the influence score.

influence(d)  =  w₁·R̂ + w₂·P̂ + w₃·D̂  =   .86
Nothing sacred about these weights — this is one way to rank evidence: cheap, deterministic, explainable. Not the only way.

sections.label: Sorted
sections.html:
SORT · THEN SHOW THE TOP

Ranked evidence, presented with the answer.

1
.86
2
.74
3
.58
4
.21
The fair win and the robotics posts carried the answer; baseball barely mattered. Answer + receipts, ranked.

sections.label: It's Real
sections.html:
IT’S REAL — AND IT’S SWAPPABLE

One reference implementation, not the way.

agentfootprint / debug
// the shipped ranking — the default scorer import { localizeContextBug } from 'agentfootprint/debug'; await localizeContextBug({ artifacts, embedder }); // your ranking — same rail, swapped scorer await localizeContextBug({ artifacts, embedder, scorer: myScorer, // any InfluenceScorer });
MANY WAYS TO SCORE
the shipped four-signal composite — the default
contrastive — score against a reference answer
learned — bring a model of your own
yours — any ranking that helps a human decide
The seam is the statement: the ranking is swappable; showing your work is not.
agentfootprint ships the seam: localizeContextBug({ scorer }) accepts any InfluenceScorer. The score you watched assemble is the default — not the definition.

sections.html:
